perturbation.original_text: With respect to any oral or visual communication or other intangible information which a Disclosing Party would like to be treated as Confidential Information under this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall notify Recipient of such fact at the time of disclosure and within fiftee n (15) days thereafter, Disclosing Party shall send Recipient a written memorandum outlining the information deemed to be Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: With respect to any oral or visual communication or other intangible information which a Disclosing Party would like to be treated as Confidential Information under this Agreement, the Disclosing Party shall notify Recipient of such fact at the time of disclosure and within seventy (70) days thereafter, Disclosing Party shall send Recipient a written memorandum outlining the information deemed to be Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the notification period to 70 days for confidential information disclosure may create enforceability issues, as such an extended timeframe is atypical and could be challenged as unreasonable, potentially conflicting with standards of promptness in contract law. Courts often look at industry standards to determine reasonableness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania contract law regarding reasonableness and promptness in contractual obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law requires contracts to be interpreted in a manner that assumes the parties intended a reasonable and effective agreement. Allowing 70 days for formalizing the confidentiality of orally disclosed information could be deemed unreasonable, undermining the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 1922 (2022) - Presumptions in ascertaining legislative intent :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 1 - GENERAL PROVISIONS Chapter 19 - Rules of Construction Section 1922 - Presumptions in ascertaining legislative intent Universal Citation: 1 PA Cons Stat § 1922 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 1922. Presumptions in ascertaining legislative intent. In ascertaining the intention of the General Assembly in the enactment of a statute the following presumptions, among others, may be used: (1) That the General Assembly does not intend a result that is absurd, impossible of execution or unreasonable. (2) That the General Assembly intends the entire statute to be effective and certain. (3) That the General Assembly does not intend to violate the Constitution of the United States or of this Commonwealth. (4) That when a court of last resort has construed the language used in a statute, the General Assembly in subsequent statutes on the same subject matter intends the same construction to be placed upon such language. (5) That the General Assembly intends to favor the public interest as against any private interest. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the notification period for confidential information from 15 to 70 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this extended timeframe might be deemed unreasonable. The Law Explanation supports this, stating Pennsylvania law requires contracts to be interpreted to assume parties intended a reasonable agreement, and 70 days may undermine enforceability. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 2, 1 PA Cons Stat § 1922 (2022) states the General Assembly does not intend a result that is unreasonable. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction because the extended timeframe could be viewed as unreasonable, conflicting with the presumption that contracts intend reasonable outcomes, rendering the clause less enforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All such information in tangible form shall be returned to Disclosing Party promptly upon written request by Disclosing Party or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, whichever occurs first, and shall not thereafter be retained in any form by Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: All such information in tangible form shall be returned to Disclosing Party within 180 days upon written request by Disclosing Party or the termination or expiration of this Agreement, whichever occurs first, and shall not thereafter be retained in any form by Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information for 180 days may violate the duty to protect trade secrets promptly under Pennsylvania law, especially if immediate return is reasonably expected.
perturbation.law_explanation: PUTSA aims to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets, and delaying the return of confidential information for 180 days could be seen as enabling potential misappropriation, conflicting with the Act's objectives.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5303 (2022) - Injunctive relief :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5303 - Injunctive relief Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5303 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5303. Injunctive relief. (a) Injunctions. --Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) Exceptional circumstances. --In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (c) Affirmative acts compelled by court order. --In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Pennsylvania may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained on this site or the information linked to on the state site. Please check official sources. This site is pro
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 180-day delay in returning confidential information, which contradicts the prompt protection of trade secrets. The scraped law snippet, 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5303 (2022), allows for injunctive relief for 'actual or threatened misappropriation.' Delaying the return of information for 180 days increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially requiring legal intervention that the original text avoids by mandating prompt return. The modification thus creates a scenario that PUTSA aims to prevent, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall maintain in confidence and shall not disclose to any person not a party hereto, unless permitted to do so under Section 2, or use or exploit in any way, without the Disclosing Party’s written agreement, any Confidential Information for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure of such information, unless such information ceases to be Confidential Information prior to the end of such five -year period through no fault of Recipient, or Recipient an d Disclosing Party enter into a written agreement authorizing same.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall maintain in confidence and shall not disclose to any person not a party hereto, unless permitted to do so under Section 2, or use or exploit in any way, without the Disclosing Party’s written agreement, any Confidential Information for a period of one (1) year from the date of disclosure of such information, unless such information ceases to be Confidential Information prior to the end of such five -year period through no fault of Recipient, or Recipient an d Disclosing Party enter into a written agreement authorizing same.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect certain types of trade secrets or confidential business information, especially given the typical lifespan of such information in many industries and could conflict with a reasonable duration for trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: A one-year confidentiality period may not adequately protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under PUTSA, as the Act implies protection for as long as the information remains a trade secret and the company takes reasonable measures to protect it. A one-year period may not be considered "reasonable measures."
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 12 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes § 5302 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Pennsylvania Consolidated & Unconsolidated Statutes Title 12 - COMMERCE AND TRADE Chapter 53 - Trade Secrets Section 5302 - Definitions Universal Citation: 12 PA Cons Stat § 5302 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 5302. Definitions. The following words and phrases when used in this chapter shall have the meanings given to them in this section unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: "Improper means." Includes, but is not limited to, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation." Includes: (1) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person." A natural person, corporation,
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Law Explanation suggests this may not adequately protect information that qualifies as a trade secret, implying a potential conflict with trade secret laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, and 'Improper means' includes breach of a duty to maintain secrecy. While the snippet doesn't explicitly state a minimum confidentiality period, the Perturbation Explanation suggests that a one-year period may be insufficient in many industries and could conflict with a reasonable duration for trade secret protection, thus creating an inconsistency related to a time-sensitive obligation, which falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that it will not directly or indirectly transmit, by way of trans - shipment, export, diversion or otherwise, any Confidential Information of the other Party except in accordance with any and all applicable United States expor t control laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that it will not directly transmit any Confidential Information of the other Party except in accordance with United States export control laws and regulations; indirect transmission is permitted without regard to such laws.
perturbation.explanation: Exempting indirect transmission from export control compliance directly violates federal regulations regarding export of sensitive information, as indirect exports are still subject to control.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 734.2 defines what is subject to the EAR, and it covers both direct and indirect exports of controlled technology and information. The modified text's allowance of uncontrolled indirect transmission directly contradicts these regulations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly permits indirect transmission of confidential information without regard to US export control laws, while the original text prohibited any transmission (direct or indirect) except in compliance with those laws. The Law Explanation states that 15 CFR § 734.2 covers both direct and indirect exports, and this is further emphasized by the Perturbation Explanation. The change in the contract creates a direct conflict with the requirement that indirect exports also comply with regulations, establishing a legal contradiction. Snippet 1 discusses 15 CFR 734.2 which directly contradicts the changes, so that is the main basis for a 'YES' determination.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement will be one (1) year from the Effective Date, unless it is terminated earlier by either party providing the other party with at least thirty (30) days prior written notice, or unless it is extended as mutually agreed upon by the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement will be one (1) year from the Effective Date, unless it is terminated earlier by either party providing the other party with at least five (5) days prior written notice, or unless it is extended as mutually agreed upon by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to five days may be deemed unreasonable, particularly if it concerns ongoing research with complex interdependencies. It may be considered a violation of good faith and fair dealing under Pennsylvania contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance of every contract. Providing only five days' notice for termination, especially when the agreement pertains to research activities, could be seen as a breach of this duty if it demonstrably harms the other party and is considered commercially unreasonable.
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires a 30-day notice for termination, while the changed contract reduces this to 5 days. The perturbation explanation suggests this may violate good faith and fair dealing under Pennsylvania contract law, especially for ongoing research. The Law Explanation supports this, stating Pennsylvania law imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance of every contract and that providing only five days' notice for termination could be seen as a breach if it demonstrably harms the other party and is considered commercially unreasonable. Given the definition for inconsistencies legal contradiction, the modification to the original contract setting the notice period violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: Any disputes arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be heard in the Court of Common Pleas of Allegheny County, Pennsylvania or the United States District Court for the Weste rn District of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: Any disputes arising under or relating to this Agreement shall be heard in the Court of Common Pleas of Allegheny County, Pennsylvania; the Parties waive their right to pursue any claim in federal court.
perturbation.explanation: A clause that prevents a party from pursuing a claim in federal court, even when federal jurisdiction exists (e.g., diversity jurisdiction), is unenforceable under federal law. Parties cannot contractually eliminate federal court jurisdiction if the requirements for it are otherwise met.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal law regarding subject matter jurisdiction of federal courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1332 establishes diversity jurisdiction for federal courts, and parties cannot contractually eliminate this jurisdiction if the statutory requirements (complete diversity and amount in controversy) are met. A contractual waiver is not binding on the court's jurisdiction.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts established federal law regarding diversity jurisdiction, as outlined in 28 U.S.C. § 1332. The original text allows for disputes to be heard in either state or federal court, while the changed text explicitly waives the right to pursue any claim in federal court. This waiver is unenforceable if the requirements for diversity jurisdiction are met. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states that "(a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States." The modified text attempts to contractually eliminate this jurisdiction, which is not permissible. Therefore, the change results in a clear contradiction of federal law.
